chunk_1.content:
Table 3: Variations on the Transformer architecture. Unlisted values are identical to those of the base model. All metrics are on the English-to-German translation development set, newstest2013. Listed perplexities are per-wordpiece, according to our byte-pair encoding, and should not be compared to per-word perplexities.

![Table](table_images/table-4.png)

The table reports an ablation study of a Transformer-style sequence-to-sequence model, showing how architectural and regularization hyperparameters affect validation perplexity (PPL, dev), validation BLEU (BLEU, dev), and model size (params, in millions). Each row is a model variant trained for a specified number of training steps, with the “base” configuration used as the main reference point.

The left side lists hyperparameters: N is the number of layers (for the encoder and decoder stack as used in the reference implementation), d_model is the model/embedding width, d_ff is the inner width of the feed-forward sublayer, h is the number of attention heads, and d_k and d_v are the per-head key and value dimensions (so the total attention projection sizes relate to h × d_k and h × d_v). P_drop is dropout probability, and ε_ls is label-smoothing strength. “train steps” indicates the training schedule length. On the right, PPL (dev) is lower-is-better, BLEU (dev) is higher-is-better, and “params ×10^6” is the approximate parameter count in millions.

The base model uses N=6, d_model=512, d_ff=2048, h=8, d_k=64, d_v=64, dropout P_drop=0.1, label smoothing ε_ls=0.1, and is trained for 100K steps. This base achieves dev PPL 4.92 and dev BLEU 25.8 with about 65M parameters. Across the table, typical dev PPL values range roughly from about 4.3 to above 6, while dev BLEU spans roughly from the low 20s up to about 26.4, with parameter counts ranging from a few tens of millions up to over 200M.
chunk_1.refreshed_content:
Table 3 presents an ablation study of Transformer architecture and regularization choices on the English→German dev set (newstest2013). Perplexity values are per-wordpiece under the paper’s BPE scheme and are not comparable to per-word perplexities. Each row is a model variant trained for a stated number of training steps; results report dev perplexity (PPL; lower is better), dev BLEU (higher is better), and approximate parameter count (params, in millions).

Hyperparameters listed include: N (encoder/decoder layer count in the reference stack), d_model (embedding/model width), d_ff (feed-forward inner width), h (number of attention heads), d_k and d_v (per-head key/value dimensions, with total projection sizes scaling as h×d_k and h×d_v), P_drop (dropout probability), ε_ls (label smoothing strength), and the training schedule length (“train steps”).

The reference “base” model uses N=6, d_model=512, d_ff=2048, h=8, d_k=d_v=64, P_drop=0.1, ε_ls=0.1, and 100K training steps, achieving PPL=4.92 and BLEU=25.8 with ~65M parameters. Across the table, PPL spans roughly ~4.3 to >6, BLEU runs from low-20s to ~26.4, and parameter counts range from tens of millions to >200M.

chunk_2.content:
Block (A) varies the number of heads h while holding the overall model width fixed (d_model=512 and d_ff=2048 are implied from the base), which forces d_k and d_v to change accordingly. The tested settings include h=1 with very large per-head dimensions (d_k=d_v=512), h=4 (128/128), h=16 (32/32), and h=32 (16/16). Performance is best around the base-like multi-head regime: h=16 reaches dev PPL 4.91 and BLEU 25.8 (essentially matching the base), while extreme choices are worse. A single head (h=1) degrades to PPL 5.29 and BLEU 24.9, and very many small heads (h=32) gives PPL 5.01 and BLEU 25.4. This suggests an intermediate-to-high head count with reasonable per-head dimensionality is beneficial, and that collapsing to one head is clearly harmful.

Block (B) changes only the per-head key/value dimensions while keeping the head count and other base settings otherwise aligned; it reports two cases with d_k=d_v=16 and d_k=d_v=32. Reducing per-head dimension to 16 hurts (PPL 5.16, BLEU 25.1) compared with using 32 (PPL 5.01, BLEU 25.4). Parameter counts for these variants are slightly smaller than base, around 58–60M, indicating modest savings with some quality loss.
chunk_2.refreshed_content:
In Table 3, Block (A) isolates the effect of attention head count h while keeping the overall model width fixed (base-like d_model=512, d_ff=2048). Because total width is held constant, changing h forces per-head dimensions to adjust: examples include h=1 (d_k=d_v=512), h=4 (128), h=16 (32), and h=32 (16). Results indicate the best behavior is in a multi-head regime similar to the base: h=16 achieves PPL=4.91 and BLEU=25.8 (about the same as the base). Extremes perform worse: a single head drops to PPL=5.29 and BLEU=24.9, while many tiny heads (h=32) gives PPL=5.01 and BLEU=25.4. Overall, using multiple heads with reasonable per-head dimensionality helps, and collapsing to one head is clearly detrimental.

Block (B) varies only the per-head key/value sizes (d_k=d_v) while keeping head count and other base settings aligned. Two tested values are 16 and 32. Using d_k=d_v=16 degrades quality (PPL=5.16, BLEU=25.1) relative to 32 (PPL=5.01, BLEU=25.4). These models have slightly fewer parameters than the base (~58–60M), implying modest size savings at some cost in performance.

chunk_3.content: Block (C) explores depth and width trade-offs and their effect on quality and parameter count. Varying the number of layers N shows that shallower models are much worse: N=2 yields PPL 6.11 and BLEU 23.7 with only ~36M parameters, while N=4 improves to PPL 5.19 and BLEU 25.3 (~50M). Increasing depth beyond the base can help: N=8 reaches PPL 4.88 and BLEU 25.5 (~80M), slightly better PPL than base with similar BLEU. The same block also varies d_model and d_ff (and corresponding attention dimensions, shown as d_k=d_v values such as 32/32 or 128/128). A smaller model with d_model=256 performs worse (PPL 5.75, BLEU 24.5) but is compact (~28M parameters). Increasing to d_model=1024 improves substantially (PPL 4.66, BLEU 26.0) but increases size a lot (~168M). Increasing the feed-forward width also shows gains: d_ff=1024 gives PPL 5.12 and BLEU 25.4 (~53M), while d_ff=4096 improves to PPL 4.75 and BLEU 26.2 (~90M). Overall, scaling up d_model and d_ff tends to reduce perplexity and raise BLEU, with expected parameter growth; the strongest BLEU within these mid-scale variants comes from larger d_model and especially larger d_ff.
chunk_3.refreshed_content:
Block (C) in Table 3 studies capacity trade-offs by changing depth (N) and width (d_model, d_ff), along with the implied attention dimensions (d_k=d_v values shown in the table such as 32/32 or 128/128). Making the model shallow hurts substantially: N=2 reaches PPL=6.11 and BLEU=23.7 with ~36M parameters; N=4 improves to PPL=5.19 and BLEU=25.3 with ~50M. Increasing depth beyond the base can help: N=8 yields PPL=4.88 and BLEU=25.5 with ~80M parameters, slightly better perplexity than the base at similar BLEU.

For width changes, shrinking d_model to 256 reduces size (~28M params) but worsens quality (PPL=5.75, BLEU=24.5). Expanding to d_model=1024 improves markedly (PPL=4.66, BLEU=26.0) but greatly increases parameters (~168M). Increasing the feed-forward dimension also helps: d_ff=1024 gives PPL=5.12, BLEU=25.4 (~53M), while d_ff=4096 improves to PPL=4.75, BLEU=26.2 (~90M). The overall pattern is that larger d_model and especially larger d_ff improve PPL and BLEU, at the expected cost in parameter count.

chunk_4.content:
Block (D) varies regularization: dropout P_drop is tested at 0.0 and 0.2 (with other settings base-like), and label smoothing ε_ls is also tested at 0.0 and 0.2. Removing dropout (P_drop=0.0) is clearly harmful (PPL 5.77, BLEU 24.6). Increasing dropout to 0.2 is close to base quality (PPL 4.95, BLEU 25.5). Removing label smoothing (ε_ls=0.0) yields PPL 4.67 and BLEU 25.3, while increasing label smoothing to 0.2 produces PPL 5.47 and BLEU 25.7. In this set, label smoothing affects PPL and BLEU differently: ε_ls=0.0 gives better PPL but slightly lower BLEU than base, whereas ε_ls=0.2 worsens PPL but keeps BLEU competitive; the clearest negative result is overly high label smoothing harming perplexity.

Block (E) notes a change in the positional encoding approach: “positional embedding instead of sinusoids.” With that substitution, the model attains PPL 4.92 and BLEU 25.7, essentially matching the base, indicating little sensitivity between these two positional encoding choices under these settings.

The final “big” model scales up capacity and training: it uses N=6, d_model=1024, d_ff=4096, h=16, dropout P_drop=0.3, trains for 300K steps, and has ~213M parameters. It achieves the best reported development metrics in the table, with dev PPL 4.33 and dev BLEU 26.4, showing that larger width/FFN size plus longer training and stronger dropout yields the strongest overall performance among the compared configurations.
chunk_4.refreshed_content:
Blocks (D) and (E) in Table 3 examine regularization and positional encoding choices, and the table also includes a large “big” configuration.

In Block (D), dropout (P_drop) and label smoothing (ε_ls) are varied while other settings remain base-like. Eliminating dropout is strongly harmful: P_drop=0.0 gives PPL=5.77 and BLEU=24.6. Increasing dropout to 0.2 remains close to the base: PPL=4.95 and BLEU=25.5. For label smoothing, ε_ls=0.0 yields PPL=4.67 and BLEU=25.3, while ε_ls=0.2 yields PPL=5.47 and BLEU=25.7. Thus, label smoothing shifts PPL and BLEU differently (no smoothing improves PPL but slightly reduces BLEU; heavier smoothing worsens PPL but keeps BLEU relatively competitive), with particularly high ε_ls clearly hurting perplexity.

Block (E) replaces sinusoidal positional encodings with learned positional embeddings (“positional embedding instead of sinusoids”) and obtains essentially base-level performance (PPL=4.92, BLEU=25.7), suggesting limited sensitivity between these two positional encoding methods under the tested conditions.

The “big” model increases capacity and training: N=6, d_model=1024, d_ff=4096, h=16, P_drop=0.3, trained for 300K steps, with ~213M parameters. It achieves the best dev results in the table (PPL=4.33, BLEU=26.4), indicating that scaling width/FFN size plus longer training (and stronger dropout) yields the strongest performance among the compared variants.

chunk_5.content: In Table 3 rows (B), we observe that reducing the attention key size d k hurts model quality. This suggests that determining compatibility is not easy and that a more sophisticated compatibility function than dot product may be beneficial. We further observe in rows (C) and (D) that, as expected, bigger models are better, and dropout is very helpful in avoiding over-fitting. In row (E) we replace our sinusoidal positional encoding with learned positional embeddings [8], and observe nearly identical results to the base model.
chunk_5.refreshed_content: The authors summarize Table 3 as follows: (B) reducing the attention key size d_k degrades quality, implying that learning/representing attention compatibility is nontrivial and that alternatives more sophisticated than a simple dot-product compatibility function might help. (C) and (D) show the expected trend that larger-capacity models perform better and that dropout is important for preventing overfitting. (E) indicates that swapping sinusoidal positional encodings for learned positional embeddings (citing [8]) produces nearly the same results as the base model.

chunk_6.content:
## 7 Conclusion

In this work, we presented the Transformer, the first sequence transduction model based entirely on attention, replacing the recurrent layers most commonly used in encoder-decoder architectures with multi-headed self-attention.

For translation tasks, the Transformer can be trained significantly faster than architectures based on recurrent or convolutional layers. On both WMT 2014 English-to-German and WMT 2014 English-to-French translation tasks, we achieve a new state of the art. In the former task our best model outperforms even all previously reported ensembles.

We are excited about the future of attention-based models and plan to apply them to other tasks. We plan to extend the Transformer to problems involving input and output modalities other than text and to investigate local, restricted attention mechanisms to efficiently handle large inputs and outputs such as images, audio and video. Making generation less sequential is another research goals of ours.

The code we used to train and evaluate our models is available at https://github.com/ tensorflow/tensor2tensor .

Acknowledgements We are grateful to Nal Kalchbrenner and Stephan Gouws for their fruitful comments, corrections and inspiration.
chunk_6.refreshed_content:
Conclusion (Section 7): The paper introduces the Transformer as a sequence transduction (seq2seq) model built entirely on attention, replacing the recurrent layers typical of encoder–decoder architectures with multi-headed self-attention. For machine translation, the Transformer trains substantially faster than recurrent- or convolution-based alternatives and achieves state-of-the-art results on WMT 2014 English→German and English→French; on English→German, the best model surpasses all previously reported ensembles.

The authors outline future work: applying attention-based models beyond translation, extending to non-text modalities (e.g., images, audio, video), exploring local/restricted attention to handle large inputs/outputs efficiently, and making generation less sequential. They also provide the training/evaluation code at https://github.com/tensorflow/tensor2tensor and acknowledge Nal Kalchbrenner and Stephan Gouws for comments and corrections.
